meta.title: F32 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW 4 Series F32 engine — B38, N20, B48, N55, B58, S55, N47, B47 and N57. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the pre‑LCI vs LCI engine choice explained. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 4 Series F32 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned F32 engine typically costs £1,800–£9,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on engine — the B38 1.5-litre three-cylinder petrol sits at £1,800–£3,200, while the S55 M4 engine can reach £5,000–£9,000. Fitting adds £520–£1,300. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which F32 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B58 3.0-litre straight-six (440i) — widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines and used by Toyota in the Supra — delivers 326 hp, effortless torque, and proven durability. The B48 2.0-litre four-cylinder (430i) is the pick of the four-cylinder range, having solved the N20's timing chain issues. The B47 diesel is also a solid, reliable choice. All three are significantly more reliable than the early N20 petrol engines.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 4 Series F32?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain stretch on the N20 petrols (pre-LCI 420i/428i) and N47 diesel (pre-LCI 420d), water pump and cooling system failures on the B58 (440i), crank hub spinning on the S55 (M4), carbon build-up on the N55 (435i), and — unrelated to the engine — boot wiring loom fractures on all F32s.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 4 Series F32 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on the engine and model year. LCI models (2016 onwards) with B48 or B58 engines are considered very reliable — these are BMW's latest-generation modular engines with proven durability. Pre-LCI N20 and N47 engines carry the timing chain risk, so a documented chain replacement is essential. The S55 in the M4 is a robust high-performance engine but the crank hub is a known weak point on tuned cars. A well-maintained F32 with the right engine can be a reliable executive coupé.

mainEntity.name: Which F32 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but the pre-LCI N20 (420i/428i, 2013–2016) and N47 (420d, 2013–2015) without documented timing chain history are the ones we consistently advise against. The S55 M4 is a fantastic engine but budget for crank hub preventative work if you plan to tune it. For most buyers, a LCI model with the B48 or B58 represents the safest choice.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my F32 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Key warning signs: cold-start rattle (N20 or N47 timing chain), overheating or coolant loss (B58 water pump), loss of power or rough running (carbon build-up on N55), crank hub failure (S55 — engine warning light, loss of power), or persistent DPF issues (diesel engines). Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an F32 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £15,000 F32, a £3,200–£5,800 B58 recon is 20–40% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £6,000 example, the same recon is 50–95% — walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: if the lowest viable replacement cost exceeds 70% of the vehicle's current market value, evaluate scrap value first.

mainEntity.name: How long do F32 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B58 straight-six and B48 four-cylinder routinely exceed 150,000–200,000 miles with proper servicing (regular oil changes, cooling system maintenance). The N55 is similarly durable but carbon build-up requires periodic cleaning. The S55 can also be long-lived but the crank hub is the limiting factor — addressed proactively, it can comfortably reach 150,000+ miles. The N20 and N47 diesels can also reach high mileages but the timing chain must be addressed proactively.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the F32, F33 and F36?

acceptedAnswer.text: All three share the first-generation 4 Series platform and most engines. The F32 is the 2-door coupé (2013–2020, this page), the F33 is the 2-door convertible (2013–2020), and the F36 is the 4-door Gran Coupé (2014–2020). Costs vary slightly by body style due to labour access and convertible-specific components — see each generation's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW F32 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine number is stamped on the block — on four-cylinder engines (N20, B48, N47, B47) it's on the left-hand side; on six-cylinder engines (N55, B58, N57, S55) it's on the front of the block near the timing cover. Tell us your variant and approximate registration date and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW 4 Series F32 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the first‑generation 4 Series Coupé — from the 420i and 428i four‑cylinder petrols to the 435i and 440i straight‑sixes, plus the M4's S55 and the full diesel range. Real UK costs and honest reliability data for BMW's compact executive coupé. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 4 Series F32 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the first‑generation 4 Series Coupé (F32) specifically — from the July 2013 launch to the September 2020 end of production.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are [PROVISIONAL] — no dedicated Engine Page exists for any F32 engine family. Ratings are derived from triangulated specialist sources (BMW forums, reliability guides, AustralianCar.Reviews). The B58 straight‑six is widely regarded as one of the most reliable modern BMW engines — praised for its robust design and used by Toyota in the Supra. The B48 and B38 modular engines are also solid choices, having solved the N20's timing chain issues. The early N20 four‑cylinder carries the highest risk due to timing chain stretch and guide failure. The S55 is a proven high‑performance engine with good reliability when maintained.

overview.intro: The F32 is the first‑generation BMW 4 Series Coupé, launched in October 2013 as the successor to the E92 3 Series Coupé. BMW's decision to spin off the coupé and convertible models into a separate 4 Series lineage reflected a strategic shift to differentiate the two‑door variants from the more practical saloon and estate models. Built on the same L7 platform as the F30 3 Series, the F32 offered a lower, wider stance and sharper handling. Under the bonnet, buyers could choose from a comprehensive range of petrol and diesel engines — four‑cylinder and six‑cylinder, from the entry‑level 418i to the fire‑breathing M4 Competition. A May 2016 facelift brought updated engines, with the N20 and N55 replaced by the more reliable B48 and B58. Production ran until September 2020, when the G22 second generation replaced it.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW L7 (shared with F30 3 Series) • Predecessor: BMW E92/E93 3 Series Coupé/Convertible • Successor: BMW G22 4 Series • Sibling body styles in this generation: F33 (convertible), F36 (Gran Coupé).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The F32 generates consistent UK enquiries [PATTERN DATA], concentrated on 420d and 430i/440i petrol variants as these executive coupés enter affordable‑maintenance territory.

items.quote: Most dependable: The B58 3.0‑litre straight‑six (440i) — widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines and used by Toyota in the Supra — delivers 326 hp, effortless torque, and proven durability. The B48 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder (430i) is the pick of the four‑cylinder range, having solved the N20's timing chain issues. The N55 (435i) is a strong, reliable six‑cylinder option if well maintained. The early N20 (420i/428i) carries the highest risk — timing chain stretch and guide failure are well‑documented. The S55 (M4) is a proven high‑performance engine. Buyers should prioritise LCI models (2016 onwards) with B48 or B58 engines.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Risky Four — avoid without chain history
Solid Four — the N20's reliable successor
Best Petrol — BMW's most reliable modern six
Performance Icon — power comes at a cost

economicsRule.title: The F32 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,200–£5,800 B58 recon against a £6,000–£20,000 F32 sits at 16–97% of the car's value. On a sub‑£8,000 F32, that brushes our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £15,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 20–40% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

problems.title: N20 Timing Chain Stretch & Guide Failure
problems.rootCause: The N20's timing chain guides are made of plastic that degrades over time — leading to chain stretch, rattling on start‑up, and eventual catastrophic engine failure if the chain snaps.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, get the chain inspected immediately — a £650–£1,800 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,500–£4,500 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare N20 engine prices for your F32 →

problems.title: B58 Water Pump & Coolant System
problems.rootCause: The B58's water pump and cooling system components can fail, leading to coolant loss, overheating, and potential engine damage if not addressed promptly.
problems.recommendation: On any 440i, budget for water pump inspection at 60k miles — a £200–£500 preventative replacement is cheap insurance against the £3,200–£5,800 replacement that follows a catastrophic overheat.

cta.label: Compare B58 engine prices for your F32 →

problems.title: N47 Timing Chain Stretch (Pre‑LCI Diesel)
problems.rootCause: The N47's rear‑mounted timing chain receives the last oil feed and is tensioned by a plastic guide that degrades over time — cold‑start rattle for 1–2 seconds is the classic early warning.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold‑start rattle on a pre‑LCI 420d, get the chain inspected immediately — a £650–£1,800 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,500–£4,500 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare N47 engine prices for your F32 →

problems.title: S55 Crank Hub Spinning
problems.rootCause: The S55's crank hub can spin on the crankshaft — a well‑documented issue on high‑power S55 engines, particularly on tuned examples. This causes timing to slip, leading to catastrophic engine damage.
problems.recommendation: If you're buying a tuned M4 or planning to tune one, budget for a crank hub capture or pinned crank hub as preventative maintenance — £800–£1,800 now is far cheaper than the £5,000–£9,000 replacement that follows a spun hub.

cta.label: Compare S55 engine prices for your M4 →

problems.title: F32 Tailgate Wiring Loom Fracture (Non‑Engine)
problems.affectedModels: All F32 variants, particularly early 2013–2016 models.
problems.rootCause: Not an engine failure, but a common non‑engine issue on BMW coupés — the wiring loom in the boot hinge area can fracture, causing rear light failure and boot release problems.
problems.recommendation: Have the boot wiring inspected on any F32 — it's a common age‑related failure that can cause MOT issues if left unaddressed.
